nodes.name: Major Rivers
nodes.description: The world's most important rivers and their key facts

items.prompt: Which river, traditionally considered the world's longest at ~6,650 km, flows through 11 African countries and empties into the Mediterranean Sea?
items.explanation:
Traditionally ranked as the world's longest river at ~6,650 km (4,130 mi), though the Amazon may rival it depending on measurement methods
Flows through 11 countries: Tanzania, Uganda, DRC, Rwanda, Burundi, Ethiopia, Kenya, Eritrea, South Sudan, Sudan, Egypt
Formed by the confluence of the White Nile and Blue Nile at Khartoum, Sudan; the Blue Nile contributes over twice the volume
The White Nile's source is near Lake Victoria; the Blue Nile originates at Lake Tana in Ethiopia
The Aswan High Dam (completed 1970) created Lake Nasser, one of the world's largest artificial lakes at 500 km long
Ancient Egyptian civilization depended entirely on the Nile's annual floods to fertilize cropland
The Nile Delta near Cairo is one of the world's largest river deltas
items.cardFront: Longest river (traditional), 11 countries, Mediterranean

items.prompt: Which river is the largest in the world by discharge volume, carrying roughly one-fifth of all river water on Earth?
items.explanation:
Largest river by discharge volume at ~209,000 cubic meters per second — more than the next seven largest rivers combined
Length is debated: 6,275–6,992 km depending on measurement; some studies make it longer than the Nile
Drains through 9 countries: Brazil, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Bolivia, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana
The 'Meeting of the Waters' near Manaus where the dark Rio Negro and sandy Solimoes flow side by side for 6 km without mixing
The Pororoca is a tidal bore with waves up to 4 m high that can travel 800 km upstream
Manaus (population ~2 million) is the largest city on the river; Belem sits near the mouth
Has no bridges crossing it along its entire length — mostly because the surrounding areas are sparsely populated rainforest
items.cardFront: Largest by volume, South America, 9 countries

items.prompt: Which river system, combined with its longest tributary, forms the 4th-longest river system in the world at ~6,000 km and drains 31 U.S. states?
items.explanation:
Proper length is 3,766 km (2,340 mi); combined with the Missouri River (its longest tributary at 4,059 km), the system totals ~5,971 km
Rises at Lake Itasca in Minnesota and flows south to the Gulf of Mexico in Louisiana
Drains all or part of 31 U.S. states and 2 Canadian provinces — about 40% of the continental U.S.
Major cities along its banks include Minneapolis, St. Louis, Memphis, and New Orleans
The river's flow is controlled by 43 dams from source to St. Louis; 29 contain navigation locks
Mark Twain's pen name comes from a Mississippi riverboat term meaning 'two fathoms deep' (safe water)
The Port of South Louisiana near the mouth is the largest U.S. port by tonnage
items.cardFront: 4th-longest system (with Missouri), 31 U.S. states, Gulf of Mexico

items.prompt: Which river, the longest in Asia at ~6,300 km, is home to the Three Gorges Dam and flows past Shanghai, Nanjing, and Wuhan?
items.explanation:
Longest river in Asia and 3rd longest in the world at ~6,300 km (3,915 mi)
Rises in the Tanggula Mountains of the Tibetan Plateau and flows east to the East China Sea
Its basin covers one-fifth of China's land area and is home to nearly one-third of China's population
The Three Gorges Dam (completed 2006) is the world's largest hydroelectric power station; 2,335 m long and 185 m high
The dam created a reservoir allowing ocean-going freighters to navigate 2,250 km inland from Shanghai to Chongqing
Major cities: Shanghai, Nanjing, Wuhan, Chongqing, Yichang
Chinese name Chang Jiang means 'Long River'; 'Yangtze' comes from an old local name for the lower section
items.cardFront: Longest in Asia, Three Gorges Dam, China

items.prompt: Which European river passes through more national capitals than any other river in the world, including Vienna, Bratislava, Budapest, and Belgrade?
items.explanation:
Second-longest river in Europe at ~2,850 km (1,770 mi), after the Volga
Passes through 4 capital cities — more than any other river: Vienna (Austria), Bratislava (Slovakia), Budapest (Hungary), Belgrade (Serbia)
Flows through or borders 10 countries: Germany, Austria, Slovakia, Hungary, Croatia, Serbia, Romania, Bulgaria, Moldova, Ukraine
Rises in the Black Forest of Germany and empties into the Black Sea via the Danube Delta in Romania/Ukraine
More than 700 dams and weirs regulate the river and its tributaries
Johann Strauss II composed 'The Blue Danube' waltz (1867), one of the most famous pieces of classical music
The Danube Delta is one of Europe's largest wetlands and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
items.cardFront: 4 capitals, 10 countries, Europe's 2nd longest

items.prompt: Which major European river flows through Switzerland, Germany, France, and the Netherlands, forming the historical northern frontier of the Roman Empire?
items.explanation:
Approximately 1,230 km (765 mi) long, flowing through 6 countries: Switzerland, Austria, Liechtenstein, Germany, France, Netherlands
With the Danube, formed the northern inland frontier of the Roman Empire
Major cities along its course: Basel, Strasbourg, Cologne, Bonn, Duisburg, Rotterdam
One of the busiest commercial waterways in the world: over 1,600 barges traverse it daily carrying 500+ million tonnes annually
The Rhine Gorge in Germany is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, famous for its castles and the Lorelei rock
About 20% of the world's chemical industry is located along its banks
Provides drinking water for approximately 20 million people
items.cardFront: 6 countries, Roman frontier, busiest waterway

items.prompt: Which river, sacred to Hinduism and personified as a goddess, flows from the Himalayas through India and Bangladesh to the Bay of Bengal?
items.explanation:
Length ~2,525–2,700 km (1,569–1,680 mi), flowing from the Himalayas to the Bay of Bengal
Considered the holiest river in Hinduism; personified as the goddess Ganga
Varanasi (Benares), one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited cities, sits on its banks with 80+ ghats (stone steps)
Hindus believe cremation at Varanasi's Manikarnika Ghat and immersion of ashes in the Ganges grants moksha (liberation from rebirth)
The Gangotri Glacier at 7,756 m elevation is the traditional source; the river proper begins at Devprayag where tributaries merge
Major cities: Varanasi, Allahabad (Prayagraj), Kanpur, Kolkata, Patna
The Kumbh Mela pilgrimage at the confluence of the Ganges and Yamuna in Prayagraj is the largest peaceful human gathering on Earth
items.cardFront: Sacred to Hinduism, India/Bangladesh, Bay of Bengal

items.prompt: Which river, the 3rd longest in Asia, flows through six countries from the Tibetan Plateau to the South China Sea, supporting the world's largest inland fishery?
items.explanation:
12th-longest river in the world and 3rd-longest in Asia at ~4,909 km (3,050 mi)
Flows through 6 countries: China (where it is called the Lancang), Myanmar, Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam
Two national capitals sit on its banks: Vientiane (Laos) and Phnom Penh (Cambodia)
Supports the world's largest inland fishery — 25% of the global freshwater catch comes from its waters
~65 million people in the Lower Mekong Basin depend on the river for their livelihoods
China has built 7+ major dams on the Upper Mekong, causing significant ecological and geopolitical tension downstream
The Mekong Delta in Vietnam is one of the world's most productive agricultural regions, known for rice and floating markets
items.cardFront: 6 countries, Tibet to South China Sea, largest inland fishery

items.prompt: Which river is the world's deepest (measured at 220 m) and the only major river to cross the equator twice?
items.explanation:
World's deepest recorded river at ~220 m (720 ft) and the only major river to cross the equator twice
Second-longest river in Africa and 9th-longest in the world at ~4,700 km (2,900 mi) including tributaries
Second-largest river by discharge volume after the Amazon; ahead of the Ganges-Brahmaputra system
Kinshasa (DRC) and Brazzaville (Republic of Congo) sit on opposite banks of Malebo Pool — the closest two national capitals in the world
The Livingstone Falls are a series of 32 cataracts spanning 354 km with a 270 m drop, making the lower river unnavigable
Because its basin spans both sides of the equator, it has stable year-round flow (always rainy season somewhere)
Its basin covers ~4 million km², about 13% of Africa's entire landmass
items.cardFront: Deepest river, crosses equator twice, Africa

items.prompt: Which river is the longest in Europe at ~3,530 km, flowing entirely within Russia and emptying into the Caspian Sea?
items.explanation:
Longest river in Europe at ~3,531 km (2,194 mi) and the longest endorheic (internally draining) river in the world
Rises in the Valdai Hills northwest of Moscow and empties into the Caspian Sea below Astrakhan at 28 m below sea level
Flows entirely within Russia, passing through major cities: Tver, Nizhny Novgorod, Kazan, Samara, Volgograd, Astrakhan
Eight massive Soviet-era dams created the world's largest chain of artificial reservoirs along its course
The Battle of Stalingrad (1942–43) was fought on the Volga at modern-day Volgograd — the war's major turning point
Often called 'Mother Volga' (Volga-matushka) in Russian culture and literature
Europe's largest river by discharge volume and by drainage basin area
items.cardFront: Longest in Europe, Russia, Caspian Sea

items.prompt: Which 346 km river flows through London and is the longest river entirely within England?
items.explanation:
346 km (215 mi) long — longest river entirely in England, second-longest in the UK after the River Severn
Source at Thames Head near Kemble in the Cotswolds, Gloucestershire; empties into the North Sea via the Thames Estuary
Flows past many of London's iconic landmarks: Big Ben, Houses of Parliament, Tower of London, Tower Bridge, the Globe Theatre
The Thames Barrier (opened 1984) protects London from tidal flooding; it has been closed 200+ times
The river froze regularly in the 17th–18th centuries, hosting 'Frost Fairs' on the ice; the last was in 1814
Provides about two-thirds of London's drinking water supply
The Oxford-Cambridge Boat Race, held annually since 1829, is rowed on the Thames in London
items.cardFront: London, longest in England, North Sea

items.prompt: Which 777 km river flows through Paris and empties into the English Channel at Le Havre, named after a Celtic goddess?
items.explanation:
777 km (483 mi) long — the second-longest river in France after the Loire
Rises at Source-Seine in Burgundy, 30 km northwest of Dijon; empties into the English Channel at Le Havre
Named after the Celtic goddess Sequana, worshipped by the 3rd-century-BC tribe that settled along its banks
Paris was founded on the Ile de la Cite, an island in the Seine; the city's Latin motto references the river
The Seine has 37 bridges within Paris, including the Pont Neuf (1607), the oldest standing bridge in the city
Major cities along its course: Paris, Rouen, Le Havre
The river and its canals carry most of France's inland waterway traffic
items.cardFront: Paris, Celtic goddess, English Channel

items.prompt: Which river flows through Baghdad, Mosul, and Tikrit and joins the Euphrates to form the Shatt al-Arab before reaching the Persian Gulf?
items.explanation:
~1,900 km (1,180 mi) long, rising in eastern Turkey and flowing southeast through Iraq to the Persian Gulf
Together with the Euphrates, defines Mesopotamia ('land between the rivers') — the 'Cradle of Civilization'
The two rivers merge at Al-Qurnah in southern Iraq to form the Shatt al-Arab waterway
Major cities on the Tigris: Baghdad (Iraq's capital), Mosul, Tikrit, Samarra
The ancient Assyrian capital of Nineveh (near modern Mosul) stood on the Tigris
Baghdad, founded in 762 CE by the Abbasid Caliphate, became the center of the Islamic Golden Age on the Tigris
The earliest known writing systems (cuneiform) and the first cities (Ur, Uruk) arose in the Tigris-Euphrates valley
items.cardFront: Baghdad, Mesopotamia, joins Euphrates

items.prompt: Which river, at ~2,800 km the longest in Western Asia, flows through Turkey, Syria, and Iraq and defined the western boundary of ancient Mesopotamia?
items.explanation:
~2,800 km (1,740 mi) long — the longest and most historically important river of Western Asia
Rises in the Armenian Highlands of eastern Turkey; flows through Syria and Iraq before joining the Tigris
Forms the western boundary of Mesopotamia; the Tigris-Euphrates system is where agriculture, writing, and urban civilization first emerged
Major cities: Raqqa and Deir ez-Zor (Syria), Hit and Nasiriyah (Iraq)
The ancient cities of Babylon and Ur stood near the Euphrates; Babylon's Hanging Gardens (if they existed) overlooked it
The Ataturk Dam in Turkey (completed 1990) is one of the largest dams in the world, causing water disputes with downstream Syria and Iraq
Mentioned prominently in the Bible as one of the four rivers flowing from the Garden of Eden
items.cardFront: Longest in Western Asia, Turkey/Syria/Iraq, Mesopotamia

items.prompt: Which African river is home to Victoria Falls, the world's largest sheet of falling water, and flows through six countries to the Indian Ocean?
items.explanation:
2,574 km (1,599 mi) long — the fourth-longest river in Africa and the largest east-flowing river on the continent
Rises in northwestern Zambia and flows through Angola, Namibia, Botswana, Zambia, Zimbabwe, and Mozambique
Victoria Falls (on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border) is 1,708 m wide and 108 m high — the world's largest sheet of falling water
David Livingstone was the first European to see the falls (1855); he named them for Queen Victoria
The local name for Victoria Falls is Mosi-oa-Tunya, meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders'
The Kariba Dam (completed 1959) on the Zambia-Zimbabwe border created Lake Kariba, one of the world's largest artificial lakes
Empties into the Indian Ocean in Mozambique through a wide delta
items.cardFront: Victoria Falls, 6 countries, Indian Ocean

items.prompt: Which river carved the Grand Canyon over millions of years and supplies water to 7 U.S. states despite often running dry before reaching the sea?
items.explanation:
2,330 km (1,450 mi) long — 5th-longest river in the United States
Rises at La Poudre Pass in the Rocky Mountains of Colorado; historically emptied into the Gulf of California in Mexico
Carved the Grand Canyon over ~5–6 million years; the canyon stretches 277 miles through the river's course
Hoover Dam (completed 1935) created Lake Mead, the largest reservoir in the continental U.S. (when full)
The river's water is divided among 7 U.S. states (Wyoming, Colorado, Utah, New Mexico, Nevada, Arizona, California) and Mexico
So heavily dammed and diverted that it often runs dry before reaching the Gulf of California
The 1922 Colorado River Compact dividing its water is one of the most consequential water agreements in history
items.cardFront: Grand Canyon, Hoover Dam, 7 U.S. states

items.prompt: Which river forms roughly two-thirds of the U.S.-Mexico border, stretching ~3,060 km from the Rocky Mountains to the Gulf of Mexico?
items.explanation:
~3,060 km (1,900 mi) long — 5th-longest river in North America and 20th-longest in the world
Rises at 3,900 m elevation in Colorado's San Juan Mountains; empties into the Gulf of Mexico at Boca Chica
Forms 1,254 miles (2,018 km) of the 1,950-mile U.S.-Mexico border — roughly two-thirds of the total
Called 'Rio Bravo del Norte' in Mexico ('Bold River of the North')
Major cities: Albuquerque, El Paso/Ciudad Juarez, Laredo, Brownsville/Matamoros
Regulated by multiple dams including Elephant Butte Dam (1916, once the world's largest artificial lake) and Falcon Dam
Flows through 3 U.S. states (Colorado, New Mexico, Texas) and 5 Mexican states
items.cardFront: U.S.-Mexico border, Rocky Mountains to Gulf

items.prompt: Which river, originating near Mount Kailash in Tibet, gave its name to both a country and the ancient civilization that built Harappa and Mohenjo-daro?
items.explanation:
~3,200 km (2,000 mi) long, flowing through China (Tibet), India, and Pakistan to the Arabian Sea near Karachi
The word 'India' derives from 'Indus,' which comes from the Sanskrit 'Sindhu'
The Indus Valley Civilization (~3300–1300 BCE) arose along its banks with major cities Harappa and Mohenjo-daro
Mohenjo-daro had advanced urban planning with grid streets, public baths, and sophisticated drainage — among the world's first
Tarbela Dam on the Indus in Pakistan is one of the largest earth-fill dams in the world
The traditional source is near Mount Kailash in Tibet, sacred in Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Bon
Alexander the Great reached the Indus in 326 BCE; the Battle of the Hydaspes on its tributary was his last major battle
items.cardFront: Named India, Indus Valley Civilization, Tibet to Arabian Sea

items.prompt: Which river, nicknamed 'China's Sorrow,' has caused some of the deadliest floods in human history due to heavy silt from the Loess Plateau?
items.explanation:
China's second-longest river at ~5,464 km (3,395 mi), also called Huang He
Nicknamed 'China's Sorrow' for its devastating floods — it has flooded ~1,593 times since 595 BCE and shifted course 26 times
The 1887 flood killed an estimated 900,000–2,000,000 people, one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history
Gets its name and yellow color from the massive load of loess (fine silt) it picks up crossing the Loess Plateau
Rises in the Kunlun Mountains of Qinghai province; empties into the Bohai Sea (a gulf of the Yellow Sea)
Major cities along its course: Lanzhou, Baotou, Zhengzhou, Kaifeng, Jinan, Luoyang, Xi'an (nearby)
Often called the 'Cradle of Chinese Civilization' — the earliest Chinese dynasties arose in its valley
items.cardFront: China's Sorrow, Huang He, deadliest floods

items.prompt: Which West African river flows in an unusual crescent shape through 5 countries, including past the historic city of Timbuktu?
items.explanation:
~4,180 km (2,600 mi) long — 3rd-longest river in Africa, 14th-longest in the world
Flows in an unusual crescent (boomerang) shape through Guinea, Mali, Niger, Benin, and Nigeria
Rises in the Guinea Highlands near the Sierra Leone border; empties into the Gulf of Guinea via the Niger Delta in Nigeria
The Inner Niger Delta in Mali is the largest wetland in West Africa, expanding from 3,900 km² to 30,000 km² seasonally
The river loses nearly two-thirds of its flow between Segou and Timbuktu to seepage and evaporation
Major cities: Bamako (Mali's capital), Timbuktu, Niamey (Niger's capital), Onitsha (Nigeria)
The Niger Delta in Nigeria is one of the world's largest river deltas and a major petroleum-producing region
items.cardFront: Crescent-shaped, Timbuktu, 5 countries, West Africa
